title: NYU FLP Exam Portal
tagline: An end-to-end exam platform for NYU's Foreign Language Proficiency program — scheduling, proctoring, audio-based assessment, and certification, all in one place.
aboutHeadline: A purpose-built exam platform for one of NYU's most-administered language assessments.
blurb: The Foreign Language Proficiency exam is taken by thousands of NYU students every semester to satisfy degree requirements. The legacy process was a tangle of spreadsheets, Qualtrics forms, and manual grading. This portal replaces all of it: a single Next.js application that schedules sittings, runs proctored audio exams in the browser, captures and processes student recordings, routes them to graders, and generates certificates — with role-based access for admins, proctors, graders, and students baked in.
pullQuotes: Replacing a decade of spreadsheets, Qualtrics forms, and email threads with a single source of truth.
Browser-recorded audio, normalized server-side, routed straight to a grader's queue.
Built for the people who actually run the exam — not just the people who take it.
highlightsHeadline: Four systems doing the heavy lifting.

highlights.title: Admin dashboard & cohort management
highlights.lede: Where every exam cycle is planned, monitored, and closed out.
highlights.body: The admin surface is the control room: sittings are scheduled on a calendar, students are bulk-imported and assigned, proctor and grader rosters are managed, and live status of every in-flight exam is visible at a glance. Role-based access keeps proctors out of grading queues and graders out of student data.

shots.caption: The control room — every active sitting at a glance.
Cohorts, sittings, and roster status in one table.

highlights.title: Browser-based proctored exams
highlights.lede: The student-facing surface — quiet, linear, and deliberate.
highlights.body: Students take the exam directly in the browser, one question at a time, with a timer, attempt locking, and tab-focus tracking baked in. The UI is intentionally stripped of distraction — no progress bars, no hints, no second chances. Proctors monitor sessions in real time and can flag, pause, or terminate an attempt.

shots.caption: One question at a time. Timer top-right.
Proctors see every in-flight attempt live.

highlights.title: Audio capture & server-side normalization
highlights.lede: The hardest part of a language exam: the speaking section.
highlights.body: Students record responses directly in the browser via the MediaRecorder API. Recordings are uploaded in chunks, stitched together server-side, normalized through FFmpeg into a consistent format, and dropped into a grader-facing queue with playback controls and time-stamped scoring. The whole pipeline survives flaky wifi and mid-attempt disconnects.

shots.caption: Recordings land in a grader's queue, FFmpeg-normalized.

highlights.title: Grading, certification & reminders
highlights.body: Once all sections are graded, results are aggregated and certificates are generated on-demand via jsPDF, signed, and emailed to students through a React Email pipeline. A cron-driven reminder service nudges students before sittings and graders before deadlines — replacing what used to be a manual mail-merge job done every two weeks.

shots.caption: React Email templates, sent via cron.

galleryHeadline: The full surface area, screen by screen.
challengesHeadline: What the spreadsheet never had to deal with.

challenges.title: Recording audio in the browser, reliably, on student laptops
challenges.body: MediaRecorder behavior varies wildly across browsers, OS permissions break in subtle ways, and a single Zoom-eating-mic moment can lose a student's entire response. The fix was chunked uploads with optimistic local buffering, server-side stitching, and an explicit mic-test gate before the speaking section starts. The pipeline now survives disconnects mid-question without losing a syllable.

challenges.title: Designing role-aware UX without role-aware confusion
challenges.body: Admins, proctors, graders, and students all share the same shell, but each sees a different application. Rather than building four apps, the codebase uses a single set of route segments with permission-gated panels — driven by tRPC procedures that enforce the same checks the UI uses. The lesson was that consistency at the data layer matters more than a clever component tree.

challenges.title: Replacing a decade-old workflow without breaking the people who run it
challenges.body: The hardest part wasn't technical — it was meeting administrators where they were. Migration involved running the new portal alongside the old process for a full cycle, exporting parallel reports for cross-checking, and writing every screen so it would make sense to someone who had been doing this job since long before the portal existed. Trust got earned one sitting at a time.

tech: Prisma schema modeling for multi-role data
soft: Phased migration off a legacy workflow
Pragmatic feature sequencing under a fixed academic calendar
